title: Share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age
caption: In 2022, the share of children achieving minimum reading proficiency by the end of lower-secondary age varies widely across nations, ranging from over 80% in the highest-performing countries to under 10% in the lowest. The bar chart arranges countries in descending order of proficiency, using color-coded bars to distinguish income groups: dark green for high-income, light green for upper-middle-income, pink for lower-middle-income, and purple for low-income countries. Ireland leads the distribution with a proficiency rate exceeding 80%, followed closely by other high-income nations such as Australia, Lithuania, and Spain, all clustered above 70%. Turkey and Greece, both upper-middle-income, sit near the 65–70% range, while Moldova and Brazil — also upper-middle-income — fall between 50% and 60%. Kazakhstan and Azerbaijan, categorized as upper-middle-income, hover around 40%, and the Dominican Republic, a lower-middle-income country, registers just above 20%. Cambodia, at the far right, shows the lowest visible value, below 10%, and is colored pink to denote its lower-middle-income status. High-income countries dominate the top third of the chart, while lower-middle-income countries occupy the bottom quarter, with upper-middle-income nations scattered throughout the middle and lower segments. The visual gradient from left to right shows a steady decline in proficiency rates, with no abrupt drops or plateaus, and the color coding reveals that income groupings do not always align neatly with performance — some upper-middle-income countries outperform certain high-income ones, and some lower-middle-income countries fall below others in the same category. The most consistent visible pattern is the concentration of high proficiency rates among high-income countries on the left, and the clustering of low proficiency rates among lower-middle-income countries on the right.
